items.title: The four-step evaluation of obesity
items.content:
Screen all adults for obesity and offer intensive lifestyle counseling. The structured evaluation has four steps:
1. An obesity-focused history covering diet, physical activity, sleep, and stress (and contributors to weight gain).
2. A physical examination to determine the degree and type of obesity (BMI + waist circumference).
3. Assessment of complications and comorbid conditions.
4. Assessment of the patient's readiness to engage in weight management.

For ALL patients, obtain a fasting lipid profile, a chemistry panel, HbA1c, and blood pressure — because obesity clusters with dyslipidemia, diabetes, and hypertension.
keyPoints: History → exam (BMI/waist) → complications → readiness.
Core labs for everyone: fasting lipids, chemistry, HbA1c, BP.
Screen all adults and offer lifestyle counseling.
items.reference: Evaluation: "The four main steps in the evaluation of obesity... are (1) a focused obesity-related history... (2) a physical examination... (3) assessment of complications and comorbid conditions; and (4) assessment of the patient's readiness."

items.title: Why language matters when discussing obesity
items.content:
The word 'obesity' is highly charged and carries a pejorative meaning for many patients — being labeled this way leaves them feeling judged and blamed, unlike being told they have diabetes or hypertension. This stigma can undermine the therapeutic relationship and a patient's willingness to engage.

Therefore, the obesity-focused history should be approached sensitively, using NEUTRAL terms patients prefer — 'weight,' 'excess weight,' 'BMI,' or 'unhealthy weight' — rather than stigmatizing terms like 'obesity,' 'morbid obesity,' or 'fatness.' Compassionate, non-judgmental communication is itself part of effective obesity care.
keyPoints: 'Obesity' is stigmatizing; patients prefer 'weight'/'BMI'/'unhealthy weight'.
Stigma harms engagement and the therapeutic relationship.
Approach the history sensitively and without blame.
items.reference: Obesity-Focused History: "the word obesity is a highly charged, emotive term... Patients prefer that clinicians use more neutral words or terms such as weight, excess weight, body mass index (BMI), or unhealthy weight."

items.title: Secondary and drug-induced causes of weight gain to consider
items.content:
Most obesity is biopsychosocial/behavioral, but the history may flag SECONDARY causes worth evaluating: polycystic ovary syndrome, hypothyroidism, Cushing's syndrome, and hypothalamic disease.

Drug-induced weight gain is a common, reversible contributor — culprits include diabetes drugs (insulin, sulfonylureas, thiazolidinediones), steroid hormones, antipsychotics (clozapine, olanzapine, risperidone), mood stabilizers (lithium), antidepressants (tricyclics, MAOIs, paroxetine, mirtazapine), and antiepileptics (valproate, gabapentin, carbamazepine). Distinguish true fat gain from fluid: NSAIDs and calcium channel blockers cause peripheral edema, NOT increased body fat.
keyPoints: Secondary causes: PCOS, hypothyroidism, Cushing's, hypothalamic disease.
Weight-gaining drugs: insulin/SU/TZD, steroids, antipsychotics, lithium, some antidepressants/antiepileptics.
NSAIDs/CCBs cause edema, not increased fat.
items.reference: Obesity-Focused History: "Disorders to consider include polycystic ovarian syndrome, hypothyroidism, Cushing's syndrome, and hypothalamic disease... antipsychotic agents (clozapine, olanzapine, risperidone)... NSAIDs and calcium channel blockers, may cause peripheral edema but do not increase body fat."

items.title: What BMI and waist circumference do and don't tell you
items.content:
BMI = weight(kg)/height(m)² classifies weight status and correlates with disease risk, but its limitation is that it measures only SIZE — not body composition, fat distribution, fitness, or health. Lower overweight/obesity thresholds apply in the Asia-Pacific region, where glucose/lipid abnormalities appear at lower body weights.

Waist circumference (or waist-to-hip ratio) is a surrogate for VISCERAL fat and is INDEPENDENTLY associated with metabolic syndrome, diabetes, and cardiovascular disease. Measure it horizontally above the iliac crest — but only meaningfully when BMI ≤35 (above that, it adds little). So BMI screens at the population level while waist refines individual metabolic risk.
keyPoints: BMI measures size, not composition/fitness/fat distribution; Asia-Pacific uses lower cutoffs.
Waist circumference ≈ visceral fat; independently predicts cardiometabolic risk.
Measure waist (above iliac crest) when BMI ≤35.
items.reference: BMI and Waist Circumference: "The problem with BMI is that it only measures the size of an individual... Measurement of the waist circumference is a surrogate for visceral adipose tissue and should be performed... in individuals with a BMI ≤35 kg/m²."

items.title: Why obesity staging goes beyond BMI
items.content:
BMI alone is an imperfect measure of the disease of obesity, so staging systems incorporate complications:
• The AACE/ACE system uses ethnic-specific BMI cutoffs PLUS adiposity-related complications: stage 0 (no complications), stage 1 (≥1 mild-moderate complication), stage 2 (≥1 severe complication).
• The Edmonton Obesity Staging System (EOSS) grades patients 0–4 by morbidity/health-risk across medical, functional, and psychological domains — INDEPENDENT of BMI.

Clinical consequence: treatment intensity is driven by complications and risk, not BMI alone. Not every person classified as 'obese' by BMI needs aggressive treatment, but those with obesity-related complications should be managed proactively.
keyPoints: AACE/ACE: BMI + complications → stage 0/1/2.
EOSS: graded 0–4 by medical/functional/psychological risk, independent of BMI.
Treat based on complications/risk, not BMI alone.
items.reference: Identifying the High-Risk Patient: "the AACE and ACE guidelines advocate a simple... obesity disease staging system that is based on ethnic-specific BMI cutoffs in conjunction with assessment for adiposity-related complications... the Edmonton Obesity Staging System (EOSS)... staging occurs independent of BMI."

items.title: Why assessing readiness to change is essential before treatment
items.content:
Initiating lifestyle change when a patient is NOT ready usually leads to frustration and can hamper future weight-loss efforts. Readiness is the balance of two opposing forces: MOTIVATION (the desire to change) versus RESISTANCE (barriers to change).

A practical motivational-interviewing tool is 'anchoring': ask the patient to rate, on a 0–10 scale, how IMPORTANT weight management is to them and how CONFIDENT they feel about doing it. This quantifies readiness and opens a dialogue (e.g., 'Why a 6 and not a 3?'). Treatment decisions use shared decision-making incorporating the patient's risk, expectations, values, and resources.
keyPoints: Readiness = motivation vs resistance; don't push an unready patient.
Use 0–10 'anchoring' for importance and confidence.
Decide via shared decision-making (risk, values, resources).
items.reference: Assessing Readiness: "An attempt to initiate lifestyle changes when the patient is not ready usually leads to frustration... use the motivational interviewing technique of 'anchoring' the patient's interest and confidence to change on a numerical scale."

items.title: Realistic weight-loss goals and the treatment ladder
items.content:
The primary goals of obesity treatment are to improve obesity-related complications, comorbidities, and quality of life, and to reduce future risk — NOT to reach an 'ideal' weight. A realistic, practical initial target is 8–10% weight loss over 6 months.

Treatment always begins with LIFESTYLE management (diet, activity, behavior), then escalates to PHARMACOTHERAPY or BARIATRIC SURGERY based on BMI risk category and comorbidities. Because BMI alone imperfectly defines the disease, not everyone labeled 'obese' by BMI needs treatment — but patients with obesity-related complications should be managed proactively.
keyPoints: Goal: improve complications/QoL and reduce risk; initial target 8–10% over 6 months.
Ladder: lifestyle → pharmacotherapy → surgery (by BMI/comorbidity).
Treat those with complications proactively, not all by BMI alone.
items.reference: Goal of Therapy: "Setting an initial weight-loss goal of 8–10% over 6 months is a realistic and practical target... Therapy for obesity always begins with lifestyle management and may include pharmacotherapy or bariatric surgery."

items.title: Why diet composition matters less than calories and adherence
items.content:
Obesity is fundamentally a disease of energy imbalance, so the primary focus of diet therapy is reducing total calories — a 500–750 kcal/day deficit (or ~1200–1500 kcal/day for women, 1500–1800 for men), aiming for ~1–2 lb/week loss.

Crucially, randomized trials comparing low-carbohydrate, low-fat, and Mediterranean diets show weight loss depends primarily on REDUCING TOTAL CALORIC INTAKE and ADHERENCE, not the macronutrient proportions. So the best diet is the calorie-reduced one the patient will actually follow; composition is then tailored to taste, culture, and comorbidities. The energy-density concept (water/fiber-rich foods lower calories per gram) and meal replacements (≈7–8% loss) are useful adherence tools.
keyPoints: Total calorie reduction + adherence drive weight loss, not macronutrient ratio.
Deficit 500–750 kcal/day → ~1–2 lb/week.
Low-energy-density foods and meal replacements aid adherence.
items.reference: Diet Therapy: "weight loss depends primarily on reduction of total caloric intake and adherence to the prescribed diet, not the specific proportions of carbohydrate, fat, and protein."

items.title: Why very-low-calorie diets can drive diabetes remission (DiRECT)
items.content:
Very-low-calorie diets (VLCDs, ≤800 kcal/day with adequate protein/micronutrients) produce rapid, large short-term loss (13–23 kg over 3–6 months). They are reserved for well-motivated patients with moderate-to-severe obesity who have failed conservative approaches and have a condition that would benefit from rapid loss (poorly controlled type 2 diabetes, hypertriglyceridemia, obstructive sleep apnea, symptomatic edema).

A landmark result: in the DiRECT trial (low-energy formula 825–853 kcal/day for 3 months, then structured support), nearly HALF of patients with type 2 diabetes achieved REMISSION to a non-diabetic state off all antidiabetic drugs at 12 months. VLCDs require trained supervision with medical monitoring.
keyPoints: VLCD ≤800 kcal/day → 13–23 kg short-term loss; supervised use only.
For motivated moderate-severe obesity with a condition improved by rapid loss.
DiRECT: ~half of type 2 diabetics achieved drug-free remission at 12 months.
items.reference: Diet Therapy: "In the DiRECT trial of patients with type 2 diabetes and obesity, a low-energy formula diet (825–853 kcal/d)... At 12 months, almost half of the participants achieved remission to a nondiabetic state off of all antidiabetic drugs."

items.title: Why exercise matters most for maintaining weight loss
items.content:
Exercise alone is only modestly effective for losing weight (diet has the bigger short-term effect), but the combination of diet + exercise is the most effective behavioral approach — and the MOST IMPORTANT role of exercise is MAINTAINING weight loss.

Guidance: ≥150 min/week of moderate-intensity (or 75 min vigorous) aerobic activity for general health, but a HIGH level (>300 min/week of moderate activity) is often needed to lose weight and sustain it. Lifestyle activity (brisk walking, stairs, housework, yard work) is as effective as structured programs; reducing sedentary time independently lowers all-cause and cardiovascular mortality. Wearable step/activity trackers aid self-monitoring.
keyPoints: Exercise's biggest role is maintaining loss; diet + exercise is best for losing.
≥150 min/week for health; >300 min/week often needed to lose/sustain weight.
Lifestyle activity counts; cut sedentary time.
items.reference: Physical Activity Therapy: "The most important role of exercise appears to be in the maintenance of weight loss... A high level of physical activity (>300 min of moderate-intensity activity per week) is often needed to lose weight and sustain weight loss."

items.title: When to add anti-obesity medication
items.content:
Adjuvant pharmacotherapy is considered for patients with BMI ≥30, OR BMI ≥27 with concomitant obesity-related disease, when diet and physical activity alone have not succeeded. Drugs should always be paired with an active lifestyle program — that support increases total weight loss.

Most approved anti-obesity medications carry an indication for CHRONIC weight management, EXCEPT phentermine and other sympathomimetics, which are approved for SHORT-TERM use only. Centrally acting agents work by reducing appetite (hunger, satiety, food reward), making it easier to follow a reduced-calorie diet.
keyPoints: Indication: BMI ≥30, or ≥27 with comorbidity, after lifestyle failure.
Pair drugs with a lifestyle program for best results.
Phentermine/sympathomimetics: short-term only; others for chronic use.
items.reference: Pharmacotherapy: "Adjuvant pharmacologic treatments should be considered for patients with a BMI ≥30 kg/m² or a BMI ≥27 kg/m² who have concomitant obesity-related diseases... with the exception of phentermine and other sympathomimetics, which are only approved for short-term use."

items.title: Phentermine/topiramate: efficacy and the pregnancy caveat
items.content:
Phentermine/topiramate (PHEN/TPM) combines a catecholamine releaser (phentermine) with an anticonvulsant (topiramate, whose weight-loss effect was discovered serendipitously in epilepsy trials). In the EQUIP/CONQUER/SEQUEL trials, 1-year placebo-subtracted weight loss reached ~9.3% at the top dose — one of the more effective first-generation agents.

Key safety point: topiramate increases the risk of CONGENITAL FETAL ORAL-CLEFT formation, so women of childbearing age must have a negative pregnancy test before starting and monthly thereafter and use effective contraception. Common adverse effects: paresthesias, dry mouth, constipation, dysgeusia, insomnia.
keyPoints: Catecholamine releaser + anticonvulsant; ~9.3% loss (top dose).
Topiramate → fetal oral-cleft risk: pregnancy test + contraception required.
Side effects: paresthesias, dry mouth, dysgeusia, insomnia.
items.reference: Phentermine/topiramate: "Because of an increased risk of congenital fetal oral-cleft formation from topiramate, women of childbearing age should have a negative pregnancy test before treatment and monthly thereafter and use effective contraception."

items.title: How naltrexone/bupropion suppresses appetite
items.content:
Naltrexone SR/bupropion SR combines an opioid antagonist with a dopamine/norepinephrine reuptake inhibitor. They work in concert on the hypothalamic melanocortin system: bupropion stimulates POMC neurons to release α-MSH (appetite-suppressing), while naltrexone blocks the β-endorphin-mediated negative feedback that would otherwise shut POMC off — so together they sustain the appetite-reducing MSH signal. In the COR trials, 1-year placebo-subtracted loss was ~4–5%.

Contraindications/cautions: avoid in seizure disorders (bupropion lowers seizure threshold) and in uncontrolled hypertension (it can raise pulse and blood pressure). Common adverse effects: nausea, constipation, headache, dizziness, insomnia.
keyPoints: Bupropion → POMC/α-MSH; naltrexone blocks β-endorphin feedback (sustained satiety).
Avoid in seizure disorders and uncontrolled hypertension (raises pulse/BP).
~4–5% loss; nausea/constipation/headache common.
items.reference: Naltrexone/bupropion: "bupropion stimulates secretion of α-melanocyte-stimulating hormone (MSH) from proopiomelanocortin (POMC), whereas naltrexone blocks the feedback inhibitory effects of opioid receptors... allowing the inhibitory effects of MSH to reduce food intake."

items.title: How GLP-1 receptor agonists reduce weight
items.content:
Liraglutide (3.0 mg daily) was the first GLP-1 receptor agonist for obesity. Beyond its incretin effect (glucose-dependent insulin secretion), it inhibits gastric emptying and glucagon secretion and — most importantly for weight — stimulates GLP-1 receptors in appetite centers (arcuate nucleus of the hypothalamus, nucleus tractus solitarius of the brainstem) to REDUCE APPETITE. Molecular modification reduces DPP-4 degradation, allowing once-daily dosing.

In the SCALE trials, liraglutide 3.0 mg gave ~3.4–6.1% placebo-subtracted loss. The main adverse effects (nausea, diarrhea, constipation, vomiting) are usually mild and transient. GLP-1 RAs are contraindicated in personal/family history of medullary thyroid carcinoma or MEN 2.
keyPoints: Acts on hypothalamic/brainstem appetite centers (plus slows gastric emptying).
Liraglutide 3.0 mg ~3.4–6.1% loss (SCALE).
Contraindicated in medullary thyroid carcinoma/MEN 2; GI side effects.
items.reference: Liraglutide: "stimulates GLP-1 receptors in the arcuate nucleus of the hypothalamus, the nucleus tractus solitarius of the brainstem... to reduce appetite... GLP-1 agonists should not be prescribed in patients with a family or personal history of medullary thyroid cancer or multiple endocrine neoplasia."

items.title: Semaglutide: a second-generation drug with cardiovascular benefit
items.content:
Semaglutide 2.4 mg weekly is the first 'second-generation' anti-obesity drug — greater efficacy plus once-weekly dosing (longer half-life). In the STEP program, placebo-subtracted weight loss ranged ~6–15% at 68 weeks.

Landmark outcome data: in SELECT, semaglutide 2.4 mg reduced major adverse cardiovascular events by 20% in patients with established cardiovascular disease and overweight/obesity WITHOUT diabetes — showing weight-loss therapy itself reduces cardiovascular events. STEP-HFpEF showed improved heart-failure symptoms and exercise function. In adolescents (STEP TEENS), it reduced BMI by ~16%. GI side effects; contraindicated in medullary thyroid carcinoma/MEN 2.
keyPoints: Weekly GLP-1 RA; STEP trials ~6–15% loss.
SELECT: 20% MACE reduction in CVD + overweight/obese WITHOUT diabetes.
STEP-HFpEF: improved heart-failure symptoms/function.
items.reference: Semaglutide: "The recent SELECT trial demonstrated a 20% reduction in major adverse cardiovascular events in patients with preexisting cardiovascular disease and overweight or obesity but without diabetes who were randomized to semaglutide 2.4 mg."

items.title: Tirzepatide: dual GIP/GLP-1 agonism and the greatest weight loss
items.content:
Tirzepatide is a once-weekly dual agonist at both the GLP-1 and GIP receptors (half-life ~117 h). Notably, its GIP-receptor agonism equals native GIP, while its GLP-1-receptor affinity is ~5× WEAKER than native GLP-1; GIP action in the brain appears to act synergistically with GLP-1 to allow GREATER weight loss.

In the SURMOUNT program, placebo-subtracted weight loss at the 15-mg dose reached ~11.6–21.4% — the largest among approved drugs, approaching surgical results. GI adverse effects (nausea, diarrhea, constipation, vomiting) predominate; like GLP-1 RAs, it is contraindicated in medullary thyroid carcinoma/MEN 2.
keyPoints: Dual GIP + GLP-1 agonist; GIP synergy enables greater weight loss.
SURMOUNT: up to ~21% loss at 15 mg (largest among drugs).
GI side effects; contraindicated in medullary thyroid carcinoma/MEN 2.
items.reference: Tirzepatide: "GIP in the brain appears to act synergistically with GLP-1 receptor activation to allow greater weight loss... Intention-to-treat placebo-subtracted weight loss for SURMOUNT 1 to 4 trials at the 15-mg dose ranged from 11.6 to 21.4%."

items.title: Setmelanotide: targeted therapy for monogenic obesity
items.content:
Setmelanotide is a melanocortin-4 (MC4) receptor agonist approved for chronic weight management in patients (≥6 years) with specific MONOGENIC or SYNDROMIC obesity: Bardet-Biedl syndrome, or POMC, PCSK1, or leptin-receptor (LEPR) deficiency. It works by restoring signaling in the leptin–melanocortin appetite pathway that is disrupted in these rare disorders, directly addressing the underlying HYPERPHAGIA.

This is precision medicine for obesity — matching a drug to a specific molecular defect. Distinctive side effects include injection-site reactions, skin hyperpigmentation (MC1R effect), nausea, and spontaneous penile erections.
keyPoints: MC4-receptor agonist for monogenic/syndromic obesity (BBS, POMC, PCSK1, LEPR).
Targets the underlying hyperphagia of the leptin-melanocortin pathway.
Side effects: skin hyperpigmentation, injection-site reactions, nausea.
items.reference: Setmelanotide: "a melanocortin-4 (MC4) receptor agonist... approved in 2020... for chronic weight management... with monogenic or syndromic obesity due to Bardet-Biedl syndrome (BBS) or POMC, PCSK1, or leptin receptor (LEPR) deficiency."

items.title: How orlistat works — and why it has no systemic side effects
items.content:
Orlistat is the only peripherally acting anti-obesity drug — a lipase inhibitor that covalently blocks pancreatic/gastric lipases in the gut lumen, preventing digestion and absorption of ~30% of dietary fat (fecal fat normalizes 48–72 h after stopping). It is minimally absorbed (<1%), so it has NO systemic side effects; tolerability is limited by the GI consequences of fat malabsorption (oily stool), which diminish as patients limit dietary fat.

Efficacy is modest (~2.7–4.1% placebo-subtracted at 1 year). Because it reduces absorption of fat-soluble vitamins (D, E) and β-carotene, vitamin supplementation is recommended. Available over the counter.
keyPoints: Lipase inhibitor blocks ~30% dietary fat absorption; minimal systemic absorption.
GI side effects (oily stool) limit tolerability; ~3–4% loss.
Supplement fat-soluble vitamins (D, E).
items.reference: Orlistat: "orlistat blocks the digestion and absorption of ~30% of dietary fat... Because serum concentrations of the fat-soluble vitamins D and E and β-carotene may be reduced by orlistat treatment, vitamin supplements are recommended."

items.title: Why bariatric surgery works (beyond restriction)
items.content:
Bariatric surgery is considered for severe obesity (BMI ≥40) or moderate obesity (BMI ≥35) with comorbidities. Its benefits go well beyond mechanical restriction: bypassing the foregut changes GUT HORMONES (altered ghrelin, GLP-1, peptide YY3-36, oxyntomodulin), bile-acid metabolism, the microbiota, and vagal signaling. Loss of visceral fat improves insulin sensitivity, raises adiponectin, and lowers inflammatory markers (IL-6, TNF-α, hsCRP).

This hormonal/metabolic reprogramming explains why surgery improves type 2 diabetes so dramatically (often before much weight is lost) and reduces cardiovascular events and mortality — it is metabolic, not just restrictive.
keyPoints: Indicated at BMI ≥40, or ≥35 with comorbidities.
Works via gut-hormone, bile-acid, microbiota, and vagal changes (not just restriction).
Improves diabetes and lowers inflammation/visceral fat.
items.reference: Surgery: "The clinical benefits of bariatric surgery... have been attributed largely to changes in the physiologic responses of gut hormones, bile acid metabolism, the microbiota, and adipose tissue metabolism... altered responses of ghrelin, GLP-1, peptide YY3-36, and oxytomodulin."

items.title: Bariatric procedures and their outcomes
items.content:
Procedures fall into two groups:
• RESTRICTIVE: laparoscopic adjustable gastric banding (the prototype, now nearly abandoned due to disappointing long-term results) and laparoscopic sleeve gastrectomy (staple/remove ~80% of the greater curvature; weight loss superior to banding).
• RESTRICTIVE-MALABSORPTIVE: Roux-en-Y gastric bypass, biliopancreatic diversion, and biliopancreatic diversion with duodenal switch.

Bypass procedures produce ~28–33% total body weight loss at 12–18 months (with variable later regain) and improve type 2 diabetes, hypertension, dyslipidemia, OSA, quality of life, and cardiovascular events. A meta-analysis showed reduced all-cause mortality (OR 0.70), global mortality (OR 0.55), and cardiovascular death (OR 0.58).
keyPoints: Sleeve gastrectomy > adjustable banding (banding nearly abandoned).
Roux-en-Y/biliopancreatic = restrictive-malabsorptive; ~28–33% TBW loss.
Surgery reduces all-cause mortality (OR 0.70) and CV death (OR 0.58).
items.reference: Surgery: "These procedures generally produce a 28–33% average total body weight loss at 12–18 months... surgery was associated with a reduced odds ratio (OR) risk of global mortality (OR = 0.55), cardiovascular death (OR = 0.58), and all-cause mortality (OR = 0.70)."

items.title: Why obesity causes PCOS in women and hypogonadism in men
items.content:
Obesity's insulin resistance/hyperinsulinemia drives sex-specific endocrine consequences:
• In WOMEN, hyperinsulinemia promotes polycystic ovaries — irregular menstruation, anovulatory infertility, and hirsutism from hyperandrogenism.
• In MEN, obesity is associated with CENTRAL (secondary) hypogonadism: low circulating testosterone with LH and FSH that fail to rise appropriately to compensate (inappropriately normal/low gonadotropins).

Recognizing the central pattern in men (low testosterone + non-elevated LH/FSH) distinguishes obesity-related hypogonadism from primary testicular failure, and weight loss can improve both syndromes.
keyPoints: Women: hyperinsulinemia → PCOS (anovulation, hirsutism).
Men: central hypogonadism — low testosterone with non-elevated LH/FSH.
Weight loss improves both.
items.reference: Endocrine Complications: "In females, the insulin resistance/hyperinsulinemia... predisposes to the development of polycystic ovaries... In males, obesity is more often associated with... central hypogonadism, where low circulating testosterone is associated with levels of luteinizing hormone and follicle-stimulating hormone that do not rise appropriately."

items.title: Why obesity is a major (and rising) cancer risk factor
items.content:
Obesity is a risk factor for several common cancers — in some countries it has overtaken smoking as the greatest cancer risk factor. Quantitatively, each 5 kg/m² rise in BMI increases cancer mortality by ~10%. The largest effects are on colorectal, kidney, and pancreatic cancer, esophageal adenocarcinoma, and (in women) endometrial carcinoma.

A notable mechanism: the rapid rise in esophageal adenocarcinoma is linked to increased reflux esophagitis from the raised intra-abdominal pressure (± hiatus hernia) of central obesity. So central adiposity contributes to cancer both through metabolic/hormonal pathways and through mechanical reflux.
keyPoints: Each +5 kg/m² BMI → ~10% higher cancer mortality.
Highest risk: colorectal, kidney, pancreatic, esophageal adenocarcinoma, endometrial.
Esophageal adenocarcinoma rise linked to reflux from central obesity.
items.reference: Cancers: "as the BMI increases by 5 kg/m², cancer mortality increases by 10%. The largest effects are on colorectal, kidney, and pancreatic cancer, adenocarcinoma of the esophagus, and, in women, endometrial carcinoma."

items.title: The broad complication burden of obesity
items.content:
Obesity affects nearly every system:
• Hepatic: ectopic liver fat → NASH → fibrosis/cirrhosis/hepatocellular carcinoma.
• Cardiovascular: hypertension, dyslipidemia, coronary disease, stroke; plus heart failure (especially diastolic dysfunction/HFpEF) and atrial fibrillation independent of occlusive disease.
• Respiratory: exertional dyspnea and obstructive sleep apnea (airway soft-tissue/adipose + central hypoventilation).
• GI: reflux esophagitis (high intra-abdominal pressure) and gallstones.
• Rheumatologic: knee osteoarthritis and gout — but NOT increased rheumatoid/seronegative arthritis despite the proinflammatory state.
• Other: dermatologic (acanthosis nigricans, hidradenitis suppurativa), dementia risk, idiopathic intracranial hypertension, worse infection outcomes (e.g., COVID-19).
keyPoints: HFpEF and atrial fibrillation occur independent of coronary disease.
Knee osteoarthritis and gout — but NOT rheumatoid arthritis.
Acanthosis nigricans, hidradenitis suppurativa, idiopathic intracranial hypertension.
items.reference: Complications: "people with obesity are also at increased risk of heart failure, particularly characterized primarily by diastolic dysfunction, and of atrial fibrillation... Osteoarthritis of the knee and gout are the two most common rheumatologic conditions clearly associated with obesity... there is no evidence for an increase in rheumatoid arthritis."

items.title: Why physical fitness predicts mortality independent of BMI
items.content:
Physical fitness and sedentary lifestyle are important predictors of all-cause mortality INDEPENDENT of BMI and body composition — a fit person with a higher BMI may have lower mortality risk than an unfit person at the same BMI. This is why the obesity-focused history must include physical-activity and exercise patterns, and why physical activity is emphasized as a treatment in its own right.

It also reframes goals: improving fitness and reducing sedentary time deliver health benefits even when weight change is modest, supporting a 'health-not-just-weight' approach.
keyPoints: Fitness/sedentary behavior predict mortality independent of BMI.
Take an activity history; prescribe activity as treatment.
Fitness gains help even with modest weight change.
items.reference: Obesity-Focused History: "Physical fitness and sedentary lifestyle, in particular, are important predictors of all-cause mortality rate independent of BMI and body composition."

items.title: Why sleep and stress belong in the obesity history
items.content:
Sleep and stress are modifiable contributors that are easy to overlook. Sleep deprivation is associated with metabolic alterations in appetite regulation, sympathetic-nervous-system overactivity, reduced insulin sensitivity, and circadian disruption — all favoring weight gain. Stress contributes partly via activation of the adrenal-cortical axis (elevated cortisol) and its effects on emotional health and eating behaviors.

Therefore the history should ask about sleep regularity, duration, efficiency, and satisfaction, and about stress — ideally combining a questionnaire with an interview — because addressing these can meaningfully support weight management.
keyPoints: Sleep deprivation → appetite dysregulation, sympathetic overactivity, insulin resistance.
Stress → cortisol elevation and eating behavior changes.
Ask about sleep regularity/duration/efficiency/satisfaction and stress.
items.reference: Obesity-Focused History: "sleep deprivation is associated with metabolic alterations in appetite regulation, sympathetic nervous system overactivity, insulin sensitivity, and changes in circadian rhythm. Stress may also contribute to obesity, in part due to activation of the adrenal cortical axis."

items.title: Behavioral therapy: the engine of lifestyle change
items.content:
Because obesity is a disease of energy imbalance, patients must learn how/when energy is consumed (diet), how/when it is expended (activity), and how to integrate this daily (behavioral therapy). Lifestyle management produces modest loss (typically 3–5 kg), but cognitive behavioral techniques make it durable.

Core strategies: self-monitoring (journaling/weighing/measuring food and activity), stress management, stimulus control (smaller plates; not eating in front of the TV or in the car), social support, problem-solving, and cognitive restructuring. Goals should be SMART (specific, measurable, agreed-upon, realistic, timely), and the patient should record planned changes for review. These time-intensive techniques are often delivered by ancillary staff (e.g., dietitian, advanced practice provider).
keyPoints: Lifestyle management ~3–5 kg loss; behavioral techniques sustain it.
Self-monitoring, stimulus control, problem-solving, cognitive restructuring.
Set SMART goals; record planned changes for follow-up.
items.reference: Behavioral Therapy: "Strategies include self-monitoring techniques... stress management; stimulus control (e.g., using smaller plates, not eating in front of the television or in the car); social support; problem solving; and cognitive restructuring."

items.question: When taking an obesity-focused history, which approach to terminology is recommended?
options: Use neutral terms such as 'weight,' 'BMI,' or 'unhealthy weight' that patients prefer
items.explanation: 'Obesity' (and 'morbid obesity,' 'fatness') is stigmatizing and can make patients feel judged, harming engagement. Patients prefer neutral terms — 'weight,' 'excess weight,' 'BMI,' or 'unhealthy weight' — and the topic should be approached sensitively.
items.reference: Obesity-Focused History: "Patients prefer that clinicians use more neutral words or terms such as weight, excess weight, body mass index (BMI), or unhealthy weight, versus more perceived stigmatizing terms such as obesity, morbid obesity, or fatness."

items.question: A patient on which medication is most likely to have drug-induced weight GAIN (rather than fluid retention)?
items.explanation: Antipsychotics such as olanzapine, clozapine, and risperidone commonly cause true weight gain. NSAIDs and calcium channel blockers can cause peripheral edema (fluid), not increased body fat. Other weight-gaining drugs include insulin/sulfonylureas/TZDs, steroids, lithium, and some antidepressants/antiepileptics.
items.reference: Obesity-Focused History: "Common causes include... antipsychotic agents (clozapine, olanzapine, risperidone)... NSAIDs and calcium channel blockers, may cause peripheral edema but do not increase body fat."

items.question: Up to what BMI is waist-circumference measurement recommended as a useful surrogate for visceral fat?
items.explanation: Waist circumference is a surrogate for visceral adipose tissue and is measured (above the iliac crest) in individuals with BMI ≤35; above that, it adds little because such patients almost always have elevated visceral fat. Waist independently predicts metabolic syndrome, diabetes, and cardiovascular disease.
items.reference: BMI and Waist Circumference: "Measurement of the waist circumference is a surrogate for visceral adipose tissue and should be performed in the horizontal plane above the iliac crest in individuals with a BMI ≤35 kg/m²."

items.question: Which is a key limitation of BMI as a clinical measure?
options: It cannot be calculated without specialized equipment
It measures only body size, not body composition, fat distribution, or fitness
It overestimates risk in all populations
It is not correlated with body fat at all
items.explanation: BMI correlates with body fat and disease risk at the population level but measures only size — it does not capture body composition, fat distribution, fitness, or individual health. It remains useful for screening, but staging and waist circumference refine individual risk.
items.reference: BMI and Waist Circumference: "The problem with BMI is that it only measures the size of an individual. It does not measure body composition, distribution of body fat, health, quality of life, or any individual characteristics."

items.question: What is a realistic initial weight-loss goal for obesity treatment?
options: No weight loss; focus only on weight maintenance
items.explanation: A realistic, practical initial target is 8–10% weight loss over 6 months, which meaningfully improves obesity-related complications and comorbidities. Treatment begins with lifestyle management and escalates to pharmacotherapy or surgery by BMI/comorbidity.
items.reference: Goal of Therapy: "Setting an initial weight-loss goal of 8–10% over 6 months is a realistic and practical target."

items.question: According to randomized trials, what most determines weight loss on a diet?
options: The exact ratio of carbohydrate to fat
Reduction of total caloric intake and adherence to the diet
items.explanation: Trials comparing low-carb, low-fat, and Mediterranean diets show weight loss depends primarily on reducing total calories and on adherence — not on macronutrient proportions. The best diet is the calorie-reduced one the patient will follow, tailored to preference and comorbidities.
items.reference: Diet Therapy: "weight loss depends primarily on reduction of total caloric intake and adherence to the prescribed diet, not the specific proportions of carbohydrate, fat, and protein."

items.question: In the DiRECT trial, a low-energy formula diet in patients with type 2 diabetes and obesity achieved what notable outcome at 12 months?
options: Nearly half achieved remission to a non-diabetic state off all antidiabetic drugs
items.explanation: In DiRECT, a low-energy formula diet (825–853 kcal/day for 3 months, then structured support) led nearly half of participants with type 2 diabetes to achieve remission to a non-diabetic state off all antidiabetic drugs at 12 months — demonstrating diabetes can be reversed with substantial weight loss.
items.reference: Diet Therapy: "At 12 months, almost half of the participants achieved remission to a nondiabetic state off of all antidiabetic drugs."

items.question: What is the most important role of exercise in obesity management?
options: It is the most effective way to lose weight on its own
It replaces the need for dietary change
items.explanation: Exercise alone is only modestly effective for losing weight, but the combination of diet and exercise is most effective, and the most important role of exercise is the MAINTENANCE of weight loss. A high level (>300 min/week of moderate activity) is often needed to lose and sustain weight.
items.reference: Physical Activity Therapy: "The most important role of exercise appears to be in the maintenance of weight loss."

items.question: Anti-obesity pharmacotherapy is generally indicated at which BMI threshold?
options: BMI ≥30, or ≥27 with an obesity-related comorbidity, after lifestyle measures fail
items.explanation: Adjuvant pharmacotherapy is considered for BMI ≥30, or ≥27 with a concomitant obesity-related disease, when diet and physical activity have not succeeded. Drugs should be paired with an active lifestyle program.
items.reference: Pharmacotherapy: "Adjuvant pharmacologic treatments should be considered for patients with a BMI ≥30 kg/m² or a BMI ≥27 kg/m² who have concomitant obesity-related diseases."

items.question: Which anti-obesity drug is approved only for SHORT-TERM use?
items.explanation: Phentermine and other sympathomimetics are approved only for short-term use. The other anti-obesity medications (semaglutide, orlistat, tirzepatide, etc.) carry indications for chronic weight management.
items.reference: Pharmacotherapy: "Antiobesity medications are approved by the FDA with an indication for chronic weight management, with the exception of phentermine and other sympathomimetics, which are only approved for short-term use."

items.question: A woman of childbearing age is started on phentermine/topiramate. What monitoring/precaution is required?
options: Negative pregnancy test before starting and monthly, plus effective contraception (topiramate oral-cleft risk)
items.explanation: Topiramate increases the risk of congenital fetal oral-cleft formation, so women of childbearing age must have a negative pregnancy test before treatment and monthly thereafter and use effective contraception. Common side effects include paresthesias, dry mouth, and insomnia.
items.reference: Phentermine/topiramate: "women of childbearing age should have a negative pregnancy test before treatment and monthly thereafter and use effective contraception."

items.question: Naltrexone/bupropion is contraindicated in which patient?
options: A patient with a seizure disorder
items.explanation: Bupropion lowers the seizure threshold, so naltrexone/bupropion is contraindicated in seizure disorders (or conditions predisposing to seizures). It also raises pulse and blood pressure, so it should be avoided in uncontrolled hypertension.
items.reference: Naltrexone/bupropion (Chap. 414/420): contraindicated in seizure disorders and increases pulse and blood pressure (should not be given in uncontrolled hypertension).

items.question: GLP-1 receptor agonists for obesity should be avoided in patients with which condition?
options: Personal or family history of medullary thyroid carcinoma or MEN 2
items.explanation: GLP-1 receptor agonists (liraglutide, semaglutide) and the dual agonist tirzepatide should not be prescribed in patients with a personal or family history of medullary thyroid carcinoma or MEN 2 (rodent C-cell tumor signal). They are actually beneficial in type 2 diabetes and OSA.
items.reference: Liraglutide/Semaglutide: "GLP-1 agonists should not be prescribed in patients with a family or personal history of medullary thyroid cancer or multiple endocrine neoplasia."

items.question: The SELECT trial showed that semaglutide 2.4 mg in patients with overweight/obesity and established cardiovascular disease but WITHOUT diabetes did what?
options: Reduced major adverse cardiovascular events by ~20%
Only reduced weight with no other benefit
items.explanation: SELECT demonstrated a 20% reduction in major adverse cardiovascular events with semaglutide 2.4 mg in patients with preexisting cardiovascular disease and overweight/obesity but without diabetes — establishing a cardiovascular benefit of weight-loss therapy itself.
items.reference: Semaglutide: "The recent SELECT trial demonstrated a 20% reduction in major adverse cardiovascular events in patients with preexisting cardiovascular disease and overweight or obesity but without diabetes."

items.question: Tirzepatide achieves the greatest weight loss among approved drugs (up to ~21% at 15 mg). What is its mechanism?
options: Dual agonism at both the GLP-1 and GIP receptors
items.explanation: Tirzepatide is a dual GLP-1/GIP receptor agonist; GIP action in the brain acts synergistically with GLP-1 to allow greater weight loss (SURMOUNT trials: ~11.6–21.4% at 15 mg). Its GIP affinity equals native GIP, while its GLP-1 affinity is ~5× weaker than native GLP-1.
items.reference: Tirzepatide: "the first long-acting, weekly, injectable, dual GLP-1/gastric inhibitory polypeptide (GIP) peptide analogue... GIP in the brain appears to act synergistically with GLP-1 receptor activation to allow greater weight loss."

items.question: Setmelanotide (an MC4 receptor agonist) is specifically approved for which type of obesity?
options: Monogenic/syndromic obesity (e.g., Bardet-Biedl syndrome, POMC/PCSK1/LEPR deficiency)
items.explanation: Setmelanotide targets the leptin-melanocortin pathway and is approved for chronic weight management in patients (≥6 years) with specific monogenic or syndromic obesity — Bardet-Biedl syndrome or POMC, PCSK1, or leptin-receptor (LEPR) deficiency — addressing the underlying hyperphagia.
items.reference: Setmelanotide: "approved... for chronic weight management... with monogenic or syndromic obesity due to Bardet-Biedl syndrome (BBS) or POMC, proprotein convertase subtilisin/kexin type 1 (PCSK1), or leptin receptor (LEPR) deficiency."

items.question: A patient on orlistat should receive which supplementation?
options: Fat-soluble vitamins (e.g., vitamins D and E)
items.explanation: Orlistat inhibits absorption of ~30% of dietary fat, which can reduce serum levels of fat-soluble vitamins D and E and β-carotene, so vitamin supplementation is recommended. Orlistat is minimally absorbed and has no systemic side effects; tolerability is limited by oily stool.
items.reference: Orlistat: "Because serum concentrations of the fat-soluble vitamins D and E and β-carotene may be reduced by orlistat treatment, vitamin supplements are recommended."

items.question: Bariatric surgery can be considered at which BMI?
items.explanation: Bariatric surgery is considered for severe obesity (BMI ≥40) or moderate obesity (BMI ≥35) with comorbidities. Its benefits derive largely from changes in gut hormones, bile-acid metabolism, microbiota, and adipose metabolism, not just restriction.
items.reference: Surgery: "Bariatric surgery can be considered for patients with severe obesity (BMI ≥40 kg/m²) or for those with moderate obesity (BMI ≥35 kg/m²) associated with a number of comorbid conditions."

items.question: Which restrictive bariatric procedure has been largely abandoned because of disappointing long-term results?
items.explanation: Laparoscopic adjustable gastric banding, once the prototype restrictive procedure, has near-abandonment status because of disappointing longer-term outcomes. Sleeve gastrectomy produces superior weight loss, and restrictive-malabsorptive bypass procedures (Roux-en-Y, biliopancreatic diversion) give ~28–33% total body weight loss.
items.reference: Surgery: "longer-term follow-up has been more disappointing, leading to near abandonment of the procedure [laparoscopic adjustable gastric banding]."

items.question: A meta-analysis comparing bariatric surgery with no surgery found what effect on mortality?
options: Reduced all-cause mortality (OR ~0.70) and cardiovascular death (OR ~0.58)
items.explanation: Bariatric surgery was associated with reduced global mortality (OR 0.55), cardiovascular death (OR 0.58), and all-cause mortality (OR 0.70), along with improvements in type 2 diabetes, hypertension, dyslipidemia, OSA, and cardiovascular events.
items.reference: Surgery: "surgery was associated with a reduced odds ratio (OR) risk of global mortality (OR = 0.55), cardiovascular death (OR = 0.58), and all-cause mortality (OR = 0.70)."

items.question: An obese man has low testosterone with LH and FSH that are inappropriately normal (not elevated). What does this represent?
items.explanation: Obesity is associated with central (secondary) hypogonadism in men — low testosterone with LH/FSH that fail to rise appropriately. In primary testicular failure, gonadotropins would be high. Weight loss can improve the hormonal profile.
items.reference: Endocrine Complications: "In males, obesity is more often associated with a degree of central hypogonadism, where low circulating testosterone is associated with levels of luteinizing hormone and follicle-stimulating hormone that do not rise appropriately."

items.question: Approximately how much does cancer mortality change for each 5 kg/m² increase in BMI?
items.explanation: Each 5 kg/m² rise in BMI increases cancer mortality by ~10%, with the largest effects on colorectal, kidney, and pancreatic cancer, esophageal adenocarcinoma, and (in women) endometrial carcinoma. Obesity has overtaken smoking as the greatest cancer risk factor in some countries.
items.reference: Cancers: "as the BMI increases by 5 kg/m², cancer mortality increases by 10%."

items.question: Which rheumatologic conditions are clearly associated with obesity?
items.explanation: Knee osteoarthritis and gout are the two rheumatologic conditions clearly associated with obesity. Despite obesity being a proinflammatory state, there is no evidence of increased rheumatoid arthritis or seronegative arthritides.
items.reference: Rheumatologic Disorders: "Osteoarthritis of the knee and gout are the two most common rheumatologic conditions clearly associated with obesity... there is no evidence for an increase in rheumatoid arthritis or the seronegative arthritises."

items.question: Independent of occlusive coronary disease, obesity increases the risk of which cardiac conditions?
options: Heart failure (especially diastolic dysfunction) and atrial fibrillation
items.explanation: Independent of occlusive arterial disease, obesity raises the risk of heart failure (particularly characterized by diastolic dysfunction/HFpEF) and atrial fibrillation, the most common arrhythmia. It also increases hypertension, dyslipidemia, coronary disease, and stroke.
items.reference: Cardiovascular Complications: "people with obesity are also at increased risk of heart failure, particularly characterized primarily by diastolic dysfunction, and of atrial fibrillation, the most common arrhythmia."

items.question: Which statement about physical fitness and mortality in obesity is correct?
options: Fitness has no relationship to mortality
Physical fitness and sedentary lifestyle predict all-cause mortality independent of BMI and body composition
Mortality depends solely on waist circumference
items.explanation: Physical fitness and sedentary lifestyle are important predictors of all-cause mortality independent of BMI and body composition — which is why the history should assess activity and why physical activity is emphasized as a treatment in its own right.
items.reference: Obesity-Focused History: "Physical fitness and sedentary lifestyle, in particular, are important predictors of all-cause mortality rate independent of BMI and body composition."

items.question: The Edmonton Obesity Staging System (EOSS) classifies patients based on what?
options: Morbidity and health risk across medical, functional, and psychological domains, independent of BMI
items.explanation: EOSS grades patients 0–4 by their morbidity and health-risk profile across medical, functional, and psychological domains, INDEPENDENT of BMI — reflecting that complications, not BMI alone, should drive treatment intensity. The AACE/ACE system combines ethnic-specific BMI cutoffs with complications.
items.reference: Identifying the High-Risk Patient: "the Edmonton Obesity Staging System (EOSS), classifies individuals with obesity into five graded categories (0–4), based on their morbidity and health-risk profile along three domains—medical, functional, and psychological... staging occurs independent of BMI."

items.question: Which group of foods has LOW energy density (helping control hunger and reduce caloric intake)?
options: Potato chips, cheese, and red meat
items.explanation: Low-energy-density foods (soups, fruits, vegetables, oatmeal, lean meats) provide fewer calories per gram because water/fiber add weight without calories; people tend to eat a constant volume of food, so these foods control hunger and reduce caloric intake. Dry/high-fat foods (chips, cheese, egg yolks, red meat) are energy-dense.
items.reference: Diet Therapy: "Examples of foods with low energy density include soups, fruits, vegetables, oatmeal, and lean meats... Diets containing low-energy-dense foods have been shown to control hunger and thus to result in decreased caloric intake."

items.question: Which is an example of a 'stimulus control' behavioral strategy in obesity management?
options: Using smaller plates and not eating in front of the television
items.explanation: Stimulus control modifies environmental cues that trigger eating — e.g., using smaller plates and not eating in front of the TV or in the car. It is one of several cognitive behavioral strategies (with self-monitoring, problem-solving, and cognitive restructuring) used in lifestyle therapy.
items.reference: Behavioral Therapy: "stimulus control (e.g., using smaller plates, not eating in front of the television or in the car)."

items.question: Lifestyle management alone (diet, activity, behavior) typically produces what magnitude of weight loss?
options: A modest loss of about 3–5 kg
items.explanation: Lifestyle management typically yields a modest weight loss of about 3–5 kg versus no treatment/usual care. Greater losses generally require pharmacotherapy or bariatric surgery, which is why treatment escalates by BMI/comorbidity.
items.reference: Lifestyle Management: "Lifestyle management has been shown to result in a modest (typically 3–5 kg) weight loss when compared with no treatment or usual care."

items.question: A clinician asks a patient to rate, 0–10, how important weight management is and how confident they feel about it. This motivational-interviewing technique is called:
items.explanation: This is 'anchoring' — a motivational-interviewing technique that quantifies the patient's interest and confidence (0–10) to assess readiness and open dialogue. Readiness reflects the balance between motivation and resistance, and pushing an unready patient often backfires.
items.reference: Assessing Readiness: "use the motivational interviewing technique of 'anchoring' the patient's interest and confidence to change on a numerical scale."

items.statement: BMI directly measures body composition and fat distribution.
items.explanation: False. BMI measures only size (weight/height²); it does not measure body composition, fat distribution, fitness, or health. Waist circumference and staging systems are used to refine individual risk.
items.reference: BMI and Waist Circumference: "It does not measure body composition, distribution of body fat, health, quality of life, or any individual characteristics."

items.statement: Low-carbohydrate diets produce substantially more long-term weight loss than other diets of equal calories.
items.explanation: False. Weight loss depends primarily on total caloric reduction and adherence, not macronutrient proportions; low-carb diets give faster initial loss but similar results long-term. The best diet is the calorie-reduced one the patient will follow.
items.reference: Diet Therapy: "weight loss depends primarily on reduction of total caloric intake and adherence to the prescribed diet, not the specific proportions of carbohydrate, fat, and protein."

items.statement: Phentermine is approved for long-term (chronic) weight management.
items.explanation: False. Phentermine and other sympathomimetics are approved only for short-term use. Most other anti-obesity drugs (orlistat, semaglutide, tirzepatide, naltrexone/bupropion, phentermine/topiramate) carry chronic weight-management indications.
items.reference: Pharmacotherapy: "with the exception of phentermine and other sympathomimetics, which are only approved for short-term use."

items.statement: GLP-1 receptor agonists reduce appetite partly by acting on hypothalamic and brainstem appetite centers.
items.explanation: True. Liraglutide (and other GLP-1 RAs) stimulate GLP-1 receptors in the arcuate nucleus of the hypothalamus and the nucleus tractus solitarius of the brainstem (plus slow gastric emptying and suppress glucagon), reducing appetite.
items.reference: Liraglutide: "stimulates GLP-1 receptors in the arcuate nucleus of the hypothalamus, the nucleus tractus solitarius of the brainstem... to reduce appetite."

items.statement: Tirzepatide produces greater weight loss than GLP-1 receptor agonist monotherapy.
items.explanation: True. Tirzepatide, a dual GIP/GLP-1 receptor agonist, produces greater weight loss (SURMOUNT up to ~21% at 15 mg) than a GLP-1 RA alone, as GIP action synergizes with GLP-1 in the brain.
items.reference: Tirzepatide: "GIP in the brain appears to act synergistically with GLP-1 receptor activation to allow greater weight loss."

items.statement: The benefits of bariatric surgery are due solely to mechanical restriction of food intake.
items.explanation: False. The benefits are largely due to changes in gut hormones (ghrelin, GLP-1, PYY, oxyntomodulin), bile-acid metabolism, microbiota, vagal signaling, and adipose metabolism — metabolic effects, not just restriction. This is why diabetes often improves before much weight is lost.
items.reference: Surgery: "The clinical benefits of bariatric surgery... have been attributed largely to changes in the physiologic responses of gut hormones, bile acid metabolism, the microbiota, and adipose tissue metabolism."

items.statement: Obesity increases the risk of rheumatoid arthritis.
items.explanation: False. Despite obesity being a proinflammatory state, there is no evidence of increased rheumatoid arthritis or seronegative arthritides. The rheumatologic conditions clearly linked to obesity are knee osteoarthritis and gout.
items.reference: Rheumatologic Disorders: "there is no evidence for an increase in rheumatoid arthritis or the seronegative arthritises among people with obesity."

items.statement: Obesity in men is commonly associated with central hypogonadism (low testosterone with inappropriately normal gonadotropins).
items.explanation: True. Obese men often have central (secondary) hypogonadism — low testosterone with LH/FSH that fail to rise appropriately. In women, hyperinsulinemia predisposes to PCOS.
items.reference: Endocrine Complications: "In males, obesity is more often associated with a degree of central hypogonadism, where low circulating testosterone is associated with levels of luteinizing hormone and follicle-stimulating hormone that do not rise appropriately."

items.statement: Orlistat has significant systemic side effects because it is well absorbed.
items.explanation: False. Orlistat is minimally (<1%) absorbed and has no systemic side effects; its tolerability is limited by gastrointestinal effects of fat malabsorption (oily stool). Fat-soluble vitamin supplementation is recommended.
items.reference: Orlistat: "Because orlistat is minimally (<1%) absorbed from the gastrointestinal tract, it has no systemic side effects."

items.statement: Every person classified as having obesity by BMI requires aggressive medical treatment.
items.explanation: False. BMI alone is an imperfect measure of the disease of obesity; not all patients deemed obese by BMI need treatment. Those with obesity-related complications and comorbidities who would benefit should be managed proactively, using shared decision-making.
items.reference: Goal of Therapy: "Not all patients who are deemed to have obesity by BMI screening need to be treated, since BMI alone is an imperfect measurement of the disease of obesity."

items.statement: Semaglutide 2.4 mg reduced major adverse cardiovascular events in overweight/obese patients with established cardiovascular disease but without diabetes.
items.explanation: True. The SELECT trial showed a 20% reduction in major adverse cardiovascular events with semaglutide 2.4 mg in patients with preexisting cardiovascular disease and overweight/obesity without diabetes.
items.reference: Semaglutide: "The recent SELECT trial demonstrated a 20% reduction in major adverse cardiovascular events."

items.statement: Lifestyle activities (walking, housework) are as effective as structured exercise programs for improving fitness and weight loss.
items.explanation: True. Studies show lifestyle activities are as effective as structured exercise programs for improving cardiorespiratory fitness and weight loss; reducing sedentary behavior is also important. A high level (>300 min/week) is often needed to lose and sustain weight.
items.reference: Physical Activity Therapy: "Studies have demonstrated that lifestyle activities are as effective as structured exercise programs for improving cardiorespiratory fitness and weight loss."

items.statement: Obesity-related fatty liver can progress to NASH, fibrosis, cirrhosis, and hepatocellular carcinoma.
items.explanation: True. Obesity causes ectopic hepatic fat that can progress to NASH, then fibrosis (a cirrhosis precursor); NASH-related cirrhosis and hepatocellular carcinoma have risen in step with obesity prevalence.
items.reference: Fatty Liver Disease: "this can progress to nonalcoholic steatohepatitis (NASH), which can progress to fibrosis, which is a precursor to cirrhosis... incidence of NASH-related cirrhosis and of hepatocellular carcinoma has increased."

items.statement: Very-low-calorie diets (≤800 kcal/day) can be safely self-administered without medical supervision.
items.explanation: False. VLCDs should be prescribed by trained practitioners in a medical setting with medical monitoring and high-intensity lifestyle support, because of their intensity and potential risks. They are reserved for selected, well-motivated patients with moderate-to-severe obesity.
items.reference: Diet Therapy: "Use of formula diets should be prescribed by trained practitioners in a medical care setting where medical monitoring and high-intensity lifestyle intervention can be provided."

items.assertion: Clinicians should use neutral terms like 'weight' rather than 'obesity' when counseling patients.
items.reason: The word 'obesity' is stigmatizing and can make patients feel judged, undermining engagement.
items.explanation: Both true and the reason explains the assertion. Because 'obesity' carries pejorative connotations that harm the therapeutic relationship, neutral terminology preferred by patients improves engagement.
items.reference: Obesity-Focused History: "the word obesity is a highly charged, emotive term... leaving them feeling judged and blamed when labeled as such."

items.assertion: Waist circumference adds information beyond BMI in assessing cardiometabolic risk.
items.reason: Waist circumference is a surrogate for visceral adipose tissue, which is independently associated with metabolic syndrome, diabetes, and cardiovascular disease.
items.explanation: Both true and the reason explains the assertion. Because BMI measures only size while waist reflects visceral fat (the metabolically dangerous depot), waist circumference independently refines risk.
items.reference: BMI and Waist Circumference: "Excess abdominal fat, assessed by measurement of waist circumference... is independently associated with a higher risk for metabolic syndrome, diabetes mellitus, and cardiovascular disease."

items.assertion: The best weight-loss diet is the calorie-reduced one a patient will adhere to.
items.reason: Weight loss depends primarily on reducing total caloric intake and adherence rather than on macronutrient composition.
items.explanation: Both true and the reason explains the assertion. Since trials show calories and adherence — not macronutrient ratios — drive weight loss, the diet a patient can sustain is the most effective.
items.reference: Diet Therapy: "weight loss depends primarily on reduction of total caloric intake and adherence to the prescribed diet, not the specific proportions of carbohydrate, fat, and protein."

items.assertion: Exercise is emphasized particularly for maintaining weight loss rather than for achieving it.
items.reason: Exercise alone produces only modest weight loss, but it is most effective at preventing weight regain.
items.explanation: Both true and the reason explains the assertion. Diet drives the initial loss while exercise's strongest contribution is maintaining the loss, so activity is emphasized for the maintenance phase.
items.reference: Physical Activity Therapy: "Although exercise alone is only moderately effective for weight loss... The most important role of exercise appears to be in the maintenance of weight loss."

items.assertion: Effective contraception is required for women of childbearing age taking phentermine/topiramate.
items.reason: Topiramate increases the risk of congenital fetal oral-cleft malformation.
items.explanation: Both true and the reason explains the assertion. Because topiramate is teratogenic (oral clefts), contraception and pregnancy testing are required for women of childbearing potential on PHEN/TPM.
items.reference: Phentermine/topiramate: "Because of an increased risk of congenital fetal oral-cleft formation from topiramate, women of childbearing age should... use effective contraception."

items.assertion: Naltrexone/bupropion should be avoided in patients with a seizure disorder.
items.explanation: Both true and the reason explains the assertion. Because bupropion can provoke seizures, the naltrexone/bupropion combination is contraindicated in seizure disorders (and conditions predisposing to seizures).
items.reference: Naltrexone/bupropion: "the drug combination is contraindicated in patients with seizure disorders or any condition that predisposes to seizures."

items.assertion: Bariatric surgery often improves type 2 diabetes before substantial weight loss occurs.
items.reason: Bypassing the foregut changes gut hormones (e.g., GLP-1, PYY, ghrelin) and bile-acid/microbiota metabolism that improve glucose homeostasis.
items.explanation: Both true and the reason explains the assertion. Because the metabolic benefit derives from gut-hormone and bile-acid/microbiota changes rather than weight loss alone, glycemic improvement can precede major weight loss.
items.reference: Surgery: "The clinical benefits of bariatric surgery... have been attributed largely to changes in the physiologic responses of gut hormones, bile acid metabolism, the microbiota."

items.assertion: Patients on orlistat should take a fat-soluble vitamin supplement.
items.reason: Orlistat blocks absorption of ~30% of dietary fat, which can lower serum vitamins D and E and β-carotene.
items.explanation: Both true and the reason explains the assertion. By reducing dietary fat absorption, orlistat lowers fat-soluble vitamin levels, so supplementation is recommended to prevent deficiency.
items.reference: Orlistat: "serum concentrations of the fat-soluble vitamins D and E and β-carotene may be reduced by orlistat treatment, vitamin supplements are recommended."

items.assertion: Clinicians should assess a patient's readiness before initiating weight-loss interventions.
items.reason: Attempting lifestyle change when a patient is not ready usually leads to frustration and may hamper future weight-loss efforts.
items.explanation: Both true and the reason explains the assertion. Because premature intervention in an unready patient is counterproductive, readiness (motivation vs resistance) is assessed first.
items.reference: Assessing Readiness: "An attempt to initiate lifestyle changes when the patient is not ready usually leads to frustration and may hamper future weight-loss efforts."

items.assertion: A physical-activity history is an important part of obesity evaluation.
items.reason: Physical fitness and sedentary lifestyle predict all-cause mortality independent of BMI and body composition.
items.explanation: Both true and the reason explains the assertion. Because fitness independently predicts mortality, assessing (and prescribing) physical activity is integral to obesity evaluation and management.
items.reference: Obesity-Focused History: "Physical fitness and sedentary lifestyle... are important predictors of all-cause mortality rate independent of BMI and body composition, which highlights the importance of taking a physical activity and exercise history."

items.assertion: Setmelanotide is effective in obesity due to leptin-receptor (LEPR) deficiency.
items.reason: Setmelanotide is a melanocortin-4 receptor agonist that restores signaling downstream of the leptin-melanocortin pathway disrupted in these patients.
items.explanation: Both true and the reason explains the assertion. Because LEPR deficiency disrupts the leptin-melanocortin appetite pathway upstream of MC4R, an MC4R agonist (setmelanotide) restores the downstream signal and reduces hyperphagia.
items.reference: Setmelanotide: "a melanocortin-4 (MC4) receptor agonist... addresses the underlying hyperphagia and specific molecular mechanism of these rare genetic diseases."

items.assertion: Central obesity contributes to the rising incidence of esophageal adenocarcinoma.
items.reason: Raised intra-abdominal pressure from central obesity increases reflux esophagitis, a precursor lesion.
items.explanation: Both true and the reason explains the assertion. Because central obesity raises intra-abdominal pressure and reflux esophagitis, it promotes the metaplasia-dysplasia sequence underlying esophageal adenocarcinoma.
items.reference: Cancers: "The recent rapid increase in the prevalence of esophageal adenocarcinoma is likely related to the marked recent increase in reflux esophagitis due to the raised intraabdominal pressure... characteristic of central obesity."
